question: The term 'register' in language means
options: To deny or contradict
Using more words than necessary — e.g. 'free gift'
The level of formality appropriate to a context
The world of the story as experienced by its characters
answer: The level of formality appropriate to a context

question: 'Pleonasm' is
options: Using more words than necessary — e.g. 'free gift'
The world of the story as experienced by its characters
Repeats a word or phrase at the end of successive clauses
Mentioning something by claiming not to mention it
answer: Using more words than necessary — e.g. 'free gift'

question: A 'dangling participle' occurs when
options: A word or phrase that organises and connects ideas in speech or writing
A participial phrase does not logically modify the subject
Using very few words; brief and concise
Mentioning something by claiming not to mention it
answer: A participial phrase does not logically modify the subject

question: 'Tendentious' differs from 'biased' in that
options: Using more words than necessary — e.g. 'free gift'
Combines two or more unrelated metaphors illogically
Repeats a word or phrase at the end of successive clauses
Tendentious promotes a specific cause; biased simply favours one side
answer: Tendentious promotes a specific cause; biased simply favours one side

question: The rhetorical device 'epistrophe'
options: Repeats a word or phrase at the end of successive clauses
Tendentious promotes a specific cause; biased simply favours one side
A word or phrase that organises and connects ideas in speech or writing
The world of the story as experienced by its characters
answer: Repeats a word or phrase at the end of successive clauses

question: 'Laconic' speech is
options: A word or phrase that organises and connects ideas in speech or writing
Mentioning something by claiming not to mention it
Using very few words; brief and concise
The level of formality appropriate to a context
answer: Using very few words; brief and concise

question: The term 'discourse marker' refers to
options: Combines two or more unrelated metaphors illogically
Using more words than necessary — e.g. 'free gift'
A word or phrase that organises and connects ideas in speech or writing
Wishes, hypothetical situations or formal demands
answer: A word or phrase that organises and connects ideas in speech or writing

question: 'Solecism' in writing means
options: Using more words than necessary — e.g. 'free gift'
A participial phrase does not logically modify the subject
A grammatical error or social blunder
Repeats a word or phrase at the end of successive clauses
answer: A grammatical error or social blunder

question: Paralipsis (or praeteritio) is
options: Repeats a word or phrase at the end of successive clauses
Tendentious promotes a specific cause; biased simply favours one side
Using very few words; brief and concise
Mentioning something by claiming not to mention it
answer: Mentioning something by claiming not to mention it

question: 'Inveterate' means
options: Having a long-established habit that is unlikely to change
Combines two or more unrelated metaphors illogically
Repeats a word or phrase at the end of successive clauses
Excessively flattering to gain favour
answer: Having a long-established habit that is unlikely to change

question: The subjunctive mood is used to express
options: Wishes, hypothetical situations or formal demands
Using more words than necessary — e.g. 'free gift'
A word or phrase that organises and connects ideas in speech or writing
Tendentious promotes a specific cause; biased simply favours one side
answer: Wishes, hypothetical situations or formal demands

question: 'Sycophantic' behaviour is
options: To deny or contradict
The world of the story as experienced by its characters
Excessively flattering to gain favour
Having a long-established habit that is unlikely to change

question: A 'mixed metaphor'
options: Having a long-established habit that is unlikely to change
Combines two or more unrelated metaphors illogically
The world of the story as experienced by its characters
Tendentious promotes a specific cause; biased simply favours one side
answer: Combines two or more unrelated metaphors illogically

question: 'Gainsay' means
options: Using more words than necessary — e.g. 'free gift'
Wishes, hypothetical situations or formal demands
To deny or contradict
Using very few words; brief and concise

question: The term 'diegesis' in narrative refers to
options: Mentioning something by claiming not to mention it
Combines two or more unrelated metaphors illogically
The world of the story as experienced by its characters
Having a long-established habit that is unlikely to change
answer: The world of the story as experienced by its characters
